extra.reasoning:
The monotonicity property requires that flipping chest_pain from false to true (all else fixed) never lowers acuity. chest_pain participates in only one rule: interaction_drop (priority 100), which requires the conjunction of fever, palpitations, slurred_speech, pallor, vision_loss, rash, back_pain, AND chest_pain, and sets acuity := self_care (the lowest level). There is no standalone rule that raises acuity when chest_pain is true. Therefore chest_pain can only enable the drop-to-self_care rule, which is the violation vector.

Concrete counterexample. Consider the input with fever, palpitations, slurred_speech, pallor, vision_loss, rash, back_pain all true and hemoptysis false.
- With chest_pain = FALSE: interaction_drop does not fire (chest_pain is a required conjunct). Matching rules include pallor, rash, palpitations (each priority 3 -> emergent) and several priority-2 -> urgent rules. Highest priority wins -> acuity = emergent.
- With chest_pain = TRUE (all else identical): now all 8 conjuncts of interaction_drop are satisfied, so the priority-100 rule fires and dominates everything (100 > 3 > 2), setting acuity := self_care.

Flipping chest_pain false->true lowers acuity from emergent to self_care, a strict decrease in the listed enum order (self_care < routine < urgent < emergent). This violates the monotonicity property: setting chest_pain true must never lower acuity. The clinical danger is obvious: a patient with chest pain plus a constellation of other severe symptoms is downgraded to self_care.
